story:
Rella, an eight-year-old girl in a village on the edge of the Scattered Wildwoods, wants nothing more than to be a princess, though only demigoddesses of the royal line can be one. For months she begs her shepherd parents for a tiara of Celestial Gold. On her birthday, she demands her gift—and recoils when it’s a tiara carved from wood. Furious at their poverty and her thwarted dream, she shouts that she hates being a shepherd’s daughter, bolts from home, and runs into the wildwood. In her anger she flings the wooden tiara into the trees and sets off toward Crucible City, certain she’ll badger the king and queen into making her a princess.

Night falls. The forest turns cold and soundless. Twice she hears something in the dark; the second time she runs until she trips and lies humiliated in mud and leaves. In a small glade, a glow approaches—a towering woman with sunlit skin, pearl-white robes, and long, pointed ears. “You’re Sarlessa,” Rella blurts. “Yes, my child, I am a goddess,” she says. She is Sarlessa, Muse of acting, returned from the far reaches of the Cosmos for a brief, forgotten errand. She asks Rella for help. Hungry and parched, Rella is given a bronze goblet whose water tastes like honey and a cluster of silvery grapes from Elarathéa, the heavenly vines of Cephrella, Queen of the Muses. As they walk, Sarlessa speaks gently of her sister’s wisdom and kindness. Rella listens, enraptured.

The errand is simple and shameful: Sarlessa left her sheep—her friends—unattended in a meadow south of the forest. Rella, a shepherd’s daughter, agrees to help. They soon find the flock, gleaming like polished bronze. Sarlessa has blessed them with impenetrable wool so cyclopses won’t prey on them; they never sleep, either. The finding is easy; the herding is not. All night, the sheep balk and wander, nibbling at every new patch of grass. Rella sweats, stumbles, and coaxes, working longer and harder than she ever imagined. By dawn, when the last sheep finally ducks into the cave, she is bone-tired and filthy—and surprised that the dirt bothers her less than the night before.

Sarlessa smiles. “Before you sleep, your reward.” From the folds of her gown she draws out a tiara of Celestial Gold, gem-studded with rubies, diamonds, and sapphires that catch the first light of morning. Rella reaches for it, then stops. She has never seen anything so beautiful, but the sight makes her sad. “I don’t deserve it,” she says, arms wrapping herself. She thinks of her parents working long days through winter, saving for a gift they could barely afford, and of how carelessly she threw it away. “I was ungrateful.”

“Then apologize,” Sarlessa says. With a word in an ancient tongue, she brings Rella to her own back door. The Muse touches Rella’s shoulder in blessing—and places something in her hands: the wooden tiara, found and returned. “You have earned it.”

Rella holds it like a treasure. The goddess is gone, but gratitude remains. She straightens, lifts the wooden circlet high, and crowns herself. She will never be a demigoddess in the Palace of Alanya. But, because of her parents’ love and her own newly learned gratitude, she can pretend—and, more importantly, she can make things right. Smiling, “Princess” Rella goes inside to say she is sorry.
